title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a community radio station. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Sophie, The Breakfast Scheduler
Sophie volunteered at the community radio station because she liked the idea of hearing familiar voices on the way to work. She was given the breakfast rota, a job that sounded easy until she saw how many presenters, guests and engineers needed to overlap. [47]The first difficulty was persuading the security office to issue early-morning passes, because volunteers kept arriving before the building officially opened.[/47] She once had a poet waiting on the pavement with a guitar player and a box of pastries. [48]Her experience organising conferences helped her build a shared calendar that showed studio time, interview slots and backup presenters in one place.[/48] People teased her for adding colour codes, but stopped when fewer shows began late. Sophie says radio taught her that listeners notice gaps of ten seconds. Behind every relaxed conversation, she now hears the careful planning that made it seem effortless. Even before sunrise each day.
texts.B:
### Adam, The Oral History Interviewer
[49]Adam joined the station after a librarian who ran the local history shelf asked him to record older residents before their memories were lost.[/49] He had never interviewed anyone formally, but he liked listening to family stories. [50]He was surprised by how carefully permission forms, recording agreements and archive labels had to be completed before a conversation could be broadcast.[/50] At first he thought this paperwork might spoil the warmth of the meetings. Instead, it helped guests feel respected. Adam learned to ask one question and then leave enough silence for the real answer to arrive. His favourite interview was with a former bus conductor who remembered every stop by the smell of nearby shops. Adam says the work changed his idea of history. It no longer feels like distant dates, but like voices that might disappear unless someone takes the trouble to switch on a recorder. Unexpectedly, that mattered.
texts.C:
### Mei, The Studio Noticeboard Artist
Mei, an art student, began helping after spending weeks waiting for evening classes in the station lobby. [51]During those waits, she kept staring at a cork noticeboard covered with curled paper, expired posters and phone numbers nobody used anymore.[/51] She asked whether she could redesign it as a live guide to what was happening in the studio. [55]She created bold cards with simple icons so visitors could instantly see whether a room was recording, editing, available or too noisy to enter.[/55] [52]Her worst moment came when a cup of coffee tipped over the first finished board and stained the schedule beyond repair.[/52] Mei had to start again, this time laminating everything. She says the board changed the atmosphere more than she expected: guests stopped opening doors at the wrong time, and nervous first-time presenters seemed reassured by knowing where to go. The practical effect pleased her far more than praise.
texts.D:
### Luis, The Quiet Room Volunteer
[53]Luis offered to improve the station’s small waiting room because his grandfather, who lived alone, depended on the station’s afternoon programme for company.[/53] Luis wanted guests to feel the same sense of welcome before going on air. The room, however, shared a thin wall with the studio, so even ordinary conversation could leak into recordings. [54]His hardest task was arranging deliveries, chair repairs and cleaning during breaks when live programmes would not pick up banging or scraping sounds.[/54] He learned to move furniture with almost theatrical slowness. Presenters began sending nervous guests to him because he could explain microphones without making people feel foolish. Luis was proud when one shy caller later came in for an interview. [56]He is now writing a simple hosting guide so future volunteers can welcome guests without relying on his memory of every little routine.[/56] He says calm is part of broadcasting. That mattered greatly.

questions.question: mentions arranging early access for volunteers before a building officially opened?
questions.explanation: Sophie had to persuade the security office to issue early-morning passes.

questions.question: used experience from organising events to coordinate people and bookings?
questions.explanation: Sophie used her conference experience to build a shared calendar.

questions.question: was invited to help preserve people’s memories by someone from a library?
questions.explanation: Adam joined after a librarian asked him to record older residents.

questions.question: was surprised by the formal preparation needed before material could be broadcast?
questions.explanation: Adam was surprised by permission forms, recording agreements and archive labels.

questions.question: became interested after repeatedly noticing outdated information in a waiting area?
questions.explanation: Mei kept staring at a cork noticeboard covered with old papers while waiting in the lobby.

questions.question: had to redo a completed piece of work after an accidental spill?
questions.explanation: A cup of coffee stained Mei’s first finished board.

questions.question: wanted to improve the place because of a relative’s dependence on the service?
questions.explanation: Luis’s grandfather depended on the station’s afternoon programme for company.

questions.question: had to arrange practical work so that live recordings were not disturbed?
questions.explanation: Luis scheduled deliveries, repairs and cleaning during breaks in live programmes.

questions.question: created visual signals to prevent people entering rooms at the wrong time?
questions.explanation: Mei made cards showing whether rooms were recording, editing, available or too noisy.

questions.question: is writing guidance so future volunteers can follow established routines?
questions.explanation: Luis is writing a hosting guide for future volunteers.
